data.title: Summit C-Suite Risks Success, Akeso Wins Either Way
data.description:
Introduction

Akeso (OTCPK:AKESF) is a Chinese biotech firm focused on licensing their internal antibody designs to other biotechs. The company is an interesting case for risk tolerant investors seeking long-term opportunities. Akeso has a significant advantage over their partnered firms, such as Summit Therapeutics (SMMT), because of the sheer diversity of their pipeline. However, geopolitical risks are no joke, even when the financial opportunity is immense. The shares trade on the OTC markets or internationally in Hong Kong, so the investment is riskier than even a normal biotech investment. Many other headwinds exist, such as expansions to the scope of tariffs or US-led retaliation to biopharma products coming from China, or even potential newly devised provisions in the Biosecure Act that may prohibit US companies from licensing Chinese companies’ IP.

Due to those factors, it will take an immense amount of return potential to even begin considering investing in Akeso. This is why many favor the exposure to single-asset plays such as Summit, leading to immense valuation despite a highly narrow pipeline. But if Summit fails, then recently announced partner Pfizer (PFE) (last resort for bulls) or another biopharma will sweep in to collect the asset. This may only happen after a significant fall in Summit's share price. This is because Pfizer is only a partner for the ADC work, rather than the manufacture and commercialization of the antibody overall. Summit has a ways to go. Although, Pfizer potentially has no skin in the game (after hedging with another PD-1/VEGF bispecific from 3SBio) and has plenty of other antibodies to partner with using their Seagen ADC technology. Meanwhile, Akeso is gaining significant milestone payments and expanding their partnerships and approved pipeline with little to no development risk of a single therapeutic. It is clear that there is only one winner in this situation.

Does Summit Have the Commercialization Ability?

While the Probability of Success for ivonescimab’s approval has risen far above the typical P3 to A levels of ~67%, particularly as the drug is already approved in China and expanding rapidly, it is uncertain whether Summit holds the same capacity to commercialize as Akeso. Bob Duggan may be an adept fundraiser and VC guy, but he has often left his companies just as the commercialization process starts. One potential indicator of risk emerging is that he and other insiders account for the majority of the fundraising for the company, instead of PE or VCs lining up to invest in ivonescimab’s potential. According to Seeking Alpha filters of biotechnology and pharmaceutical companies, Summit has the seventh-highest insider ownership and is the second-largest market cap in the top 50 (Sino Biopharmaceutical Ltd. (OTCPK:SBMFF) takes first at 40th place).

Some restraint must be taken when considering Summit's opportunity only from a lens of their management team, as is a common argument. Let’s take the Pharmacyclics (ABBV) example: a small molecule therapy for a single indication. Perhaps it is hindsight that Duggan states he knew Imbruvica would be a blockbuster despite initially investing in the company in 2004/5 when the star therapy was the soon-to-be-denied Xcytrin. Duggan’s shares fell from around a $10 cost average to less than a dollar. He became the majority shareholder, and the former CEO resigned, so he stepped in. The limited clinical validation during the time he invested and became CEO did not change the science, but does suggest a fortunate turn of events as he attempted to right the ship.

These points are cogent because Imbruvica's validation was not confirmed until 2011, when Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) collaborated for $1 billion. The JNJ co-commercialization deal was a far larger sum and catalyst than Pfizer’s current deal, which had no announced upfront payment or co-commercialization terms. Pfizer's focus is to develop their ADC ligands and attach them to any antibody that may be successful in immunotherapy. Not to mention Pfizer has just unveiled seven clinical trials for their own PF-08634404/SSGJ-797 PD-1xVEGF bispecific. Pfizer is hedging based on necessity when analyzing the field. Competition in immunology is immense, and this deal creates ambiguity, as discussed by Fierce Biotech:

The launch of this trial unavoidably puts Pfizer/3SBio on a collision course with Summit Therapeutics/Akeso’s ivonescimab and Bristol Myers Squibb/BioNTech’s pumitamig, two other PD-(L)1-VEGF drugs in phase 3 testing in first-line NSCLC.

It has also been reported that despite the dire need for funding, the Pfizer deal may be cancelled as well. Per another Fierce article - one of the most recent mentioning the deal and noting how Summit did not have a quarterly earnings call in Q2 - it is likely true but extremely poor for the company:

“We continue to believe it is critical that Summit secure a partnership that will support VEGFA/PD-1 ivonescimab’s global development,” Leerink Partners analysts said in a note this week, arguing that the company lacks the means to rapidly advance the drug in combination regimens by itself.

On Monday, Summit proposed to raise up to $360 million by selling new shares of its stock from time to time. As of the end of June, Summit had cash and cash equivalents worth nearly $298 million.

The new money “could provide the company with temporary alleviation of their cash overhang, but it does not preclude the need to partner [ivonescimab],” the Leerink team said…

…Summit previously formed a clinical collaboration with Pfizer. But that partnership is dead now with Pfizer’s 3SBio deal.

Even if the deal continues thanks to Pfizer's hedged approach, this may lead to multiple approved therapies across similar indications and treatment methods, preventing ivonescimab from gaining expected market share. This will require deft care in the commercialization strategy, particularly when enticing physicians to prescribe.

To meet the task, Duggan has hired a Chief Commercialization Officer, Robert LaCaze, who has an extensive 35-year career with a background in marketing, first rising through the ranks at Bristol-Myers (BMY). Then, he took a lead oncology role at Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) before moving on to be CEO of small, French CAR-T biotech Mnemo Therapeutics. Unfortunately, Mnemo seems to have gone under since he joined in 2022 (the company website is no longer functional, VC site has no updates since 2022). He no doubt brings experience to the table, but I wonder if other former Merck, Roche, or other blockbuster monoclonal antibody experts could have been brought along to bring significance to ivonescimab's prospects.

The Summit C-suite is also composed about half by former Pharmacyclics employees under Duggan, so that exacerbates the experience difference between small molecule and biologic. The majority of fundraising is also derived from this close-knit team, leading to the high insider ownership. There is enough evidence to allow for a significant discount to be applied to Summit in terms of their commercialization ability and possibilities. I also wonder how long Duggan and Co can continue subsidizing the company, as the manufacturing requirements and costs will be extensive.

I prefer to feel comfortable with a highly experienced management team or founder-led mentality when investing in biotechs. I think smart money is now looking past the ivonescimab opportunity to a more wary scene. Seeking Alpha Wall St. analyst data also shows a growing trend of negative views in SMMT, and this will be a crucial data point to watch. Summit will face intense competition in the PD-(L)1 space, and each new entrant will eat away at ivonescimab's long-term viability. I will summarize them as follows:

  • Keytruda/Tecentriq/Opdivo generics: low cost, high prescriber comfort, can be combined with VEGF inhibitors. Will lead to pricing wars despite significant differences in efficacy.
  • Novel biomarker approaches, or personalized medicine, may be favored over broad checkpoint and angiogenesis inhibition.
  • Next-gen approaches: PROTACs, peptides, and small molecules will offer superior patient favorability with oral administration.

Therefore, regardless of the team in place, it is also a question as to whether Summit's individual focus on ivonescimab is viable. This leads me to believe that diversifying away, but keeping exposure via Akeso is a risk-adjusted path to take.

Akeso Wins the Deal

Due to the risks, I point to Akeso as the favored party if one wants exposure to a potential Keytruda (MRK) replacement. Akeso won out in the deal with Summit. The terms of the Akeso ivonescimab licensing to Summit are favorable for the Chinese biotech financially for a few key reasons. Akeso earned $500 million up front, which is quite significant for a licensing deal with a total of $5 billion possible upon all milestones being met. While one would say there is risk for Akeso since they have only earned 10% of the payment, it is important to note Akeso has retained Chinese commercialization rights and is currently approved and selling in that region. And, if ivonescimab is approved in the US, Akeso’s royalties will be in the double digits, which is very high for an out-licensed therapy with such potential.

Akeso will also not be manufacturing the therapy, so SMMT must invest in the manufacturing requirements for the US, Canada, Europe, and Japan, which is quite complex for a pre-commercial entity seeking to make more than $10 billion in peak sales. Don’t forget that Summit, when first invested in by Duggan, was in clinical trials for an antibiotic. The move from antibiotics to ivonescimab represents a paradigm shift after the initial investment failed to materialize. Based on the terms of the deal, such as high royalties and the manufacturing burden, questions are raised about whether financial willingness or commercial capability led Akeso to go with Summit.

Current Financial Status

Another benefit to preferring Akeso is that it offers far more diversification, even into equally as lucrative antibody pathways as PD-1/VEGF. There are three approved therapies that offer significant clinical validation: an anti-PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific approved in 2022 (China only, Fast Track/Orphan status in the US), an anti-PD-1 monoclonal approved in 2025 (FDA and China approved), and an anti-PCSK9 antibody for high cholesterol approved in 2024 (China only). Of the most significance, cadonilimab, or the anti-PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific, is a first-in-class molecule and accounts for over $300 million in sales over the past twelve months.

Base revenues outside of milestone payments are growing about 30% per year thanks to these approvals in China alone. If expanded to the US, and royalties from ivonescimab are included, the continued growth is assured. The pipeline is also healthy, and I expect the company to optimize their globalization strategy moving forward following the success of their FDA-approved anti-PD-1 antibody.

Akeso also has a solid balance sheet with ~$1 billion USD in reserve, well in excess of debt at $634 million. This contrasts with Summit's predicament as cash on hand falls below $300 million. Next milestones for Akeso should come upon more P3 data and a potential approval. Therefore, there will be other cash infusions for Akeso in the medium term, but this will drain Summit's coffers. Then, the commercialization process begins, and that is where the most risk exists, as mAbs have considerable capex requirements at invonescimab's expected scale and reach. But Akeso will earn the milestones and a return on most of their R&D expenses, at the least.

Conclusion

So, then the question will be whether Akeso is worth investing in at the $14 billion price tag. There have been some buy points far lower than current prices, which would have been much nicer to have, but now we must think about the future. I find that most investors were considering SMMT superior up until Pfizer hedged earlier this year. Since then, the share price of Summit has fallen by over 25%, while Akeso has risen over 50%. This pattern is likely to continue unless a tangible commercialization deal materializes.

Akeso's current valuation is the largest risk as top therapies remain out of the US market, but China's pricing for therapies is increasing with their broader economy as the country matures. Their ability to design therapies is also validated by this point, so I expect their internal pipeline to be rapidly expanded to allow the company to grow into the valuation. But, as I noted before, investors must be willing to see geopolitics interfere with the free market. So be sure to discount your valuations accordingly. Akeso will survive in China, but will you want to remain invested if they can't access the US? If you do hold Summit, there is little reason to not begin slowly selling and accumulating Akeso shares. Same scientific exposure, far less risk.

Thanks for reading. Feel free to share your thoughts below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MercadoLibre: Fintech Growth And Margin Expansion Set The Stage For A 2026 Re-Rating
data.description:
During this year I published several articles about MercadoLibre (MELI), and from the first one in April, one of the first that I wrote in Seeking Alpha, until the last one in September, I was noticing that there is a common thread, and it is that MELI went from very accelerated growth to a more balanced one. As the quarters go by, I see that the company is increasingly focused on expanding profitably and consistently.

However, this process didn't happen overnight, but each quarter brought something different, first with more scale, then with a stronger financial structure, and finally with a fintech that began to show a greater degree of maturity.

In this article I'll continue to analyze the evolution of MELI, since the results of Q3 leave us several interesting points to consider. I don't see a change of course, but a readjustment in the way it prioritizes growth. The results give me an idea that today MELI is focused on the efficiency of its investments and the organization of resources in a way that will allow it to maintain leadership in Latin America, without losing sight of profitability.

Investment Thesis

Talking about MercadoLibre is not as simple as saying it’s an e-commerce company. It was at first, of course, more than twenty years ago, but it no longer fits that category. Over time, it became much more extensive and complex. Nowadays it is an ecosystem where logistics, financial services, credit, and the way millions of people buy, pay, and manage their money in the region coexist.

Although I mentioned at the beginning that I have been writing about it for several months, I have been following MELI for several years, and something that has always caught my attention is the capacity it has to grow, even in contexts where many companies barely manage to sustain themselves. After reviewing the results, I see that this quarter maintains this dynamic, albeit with a different nuance, as while growth remains strong, margins show the impact of investing more in free shipping, marketing, logistics, and credit expansion.

Personally, I think it is a logical cost to pay for the company at this stage. And although the market can stop at a few tenths of a margin, what I think ends up defining the long-term history is the competitive position that MELI builds.

In addition, in a context where credit became more expensive and global liquidity became scarcer, it is not common to see a Latin American company grow between 35-40% annually and sustain double-digit margins. MELI combines features rare in the region, as it integrates scale, operational discipline, and a model that is supported by commerce, fintech, and logistics. All this, I believe, allows it to resist external shocks much better.

While several global platforms are cutting investments or adjusting structure, MELI continues to expand its distribution network, strengthening its payment technology and expanding a financial ecosystem that already exceeds 75 million active users. That operational depth, rather than the one-time quarter margin, I believe is what sustains long-term value. For that reason, I maintain my position of the last quarter of "buy" with a long-term horizon.

Q3 2025: Growth Unshaken, Margins Adjusting To Expansion

Q3 2025 was, once again, a display of execution. The net revenues grew 39% YoY up to $7.5 B (+49% FXN), boosted by the combination of growth in commerce and fintech. The GMW reached $16.5B (+35% FXN), while the total TPV reached $71.2B (+54% FXN). In Brazil and Mexico, the activity was record-breaking.

As highlighted by Martín de los Santos, the company’s CFO, during the earnings call:

Revenues grew 39 percent year over year, marking the 27th consecutive quarter above 30 percent growth.

The number of active buyers reached 76.8 million (+26% YoY), the highest expansion since 2021.

The income from operations was of $724MM, with a margin of 9.8%, below last quarter’s 12.2%, reflecting the investment in the program of free shipping and marketing in Brazil. The net income was placed at $421MM, with a margin of 5.7%, affected by FX losses and an even higher tax rate. Even then, the net benefit grew 6% YoY, something that few manage in a quarter of such operative expansion.

The most important reading I make of the quarter lies not only on the numbers but also on its dynamic: the growth is transversal, not focused. While the electronic trade grows 38% YoY, fintech does so at 65%, and the credit more than 80%, with controlled late payment. That proves that the expansion doesn’t depend on a single driver but on the integration of the ecosystem.

As Ariel Szarfsztejn, Commerce President, noted during the call:

We have amazing growth, higher frequency from our buyers, record conversion rates, record retention rates for new buyers, and record retention rates for existing buyers.

That is the structural difference between MELI and any other regional company.

What I highlight the most is how MELI manages to expand its credit without worsening the quality. Amid the persistent volatility of Argentina and Brazil, the company maintained stable liquidity and asset quality, showing how its scale and financial discipline already act as buffers against local shocks. Few companies can do that

The adjusted FCF was $206MM, after $375MM in Capex and $1.73B assigned to the credit portfolio expansion, partially compensated by $485MM of financing of thirds. The cash position closed at $5.27B, with an adjusted net debt / EBITDA relationship of 1.19x, still a very healthy level.

At a cash flow level, the most relevant fact is that MELI managed to hold positive generation despite a credit portfolio growth of nearly $1.8B. This implies that it finances its organic expansion without relying on external capital, an enormous advantage in the current higher–rate environment. That operative leverage – controlled and efficient – allows it to sustain margins without deteriorating balance.

Valuation, Growth and Profitability: Strength Justifies The Premium

Talking about MELI always leads to talking about more demanding prices. The P/E FWD is at 51.4x, the EV/EBITDA FWD at 27.2x, and the P/S FWD at 3.8x – way above the sectorial’s average – but between 40% and 80% below its five-year historical numbers. In other words, it’s still expensive, but less than what MELI used to be.

And, truthfully, it’s worth it. The revenue growth, near 37% YoY, the EBITDA is 41%, and the EPS diluted is 46%, while the consensus projects a EPS growth of 32% in the next 3-5 years CAGR.

The margins remain solid: EBIT 12%, EBITDA 14.7%, net 7.9%, and an ROE of 40.6%, nearly four times higher than the sector’s average.

Yes, the FCF margin remains negative (–15.5%), but not for weakness, but for expansion: MELI is aggressively reinvesting in its platform, credit, and logistics, cautiously financed and without diluting capital.

I prefer to see that as a sign of strength, not of weakness: it's building an infrastructure that its competitors can barely mimic.

If we compare numbers, MELI’s P/E FWD rounds to 51x against Amazon’s (AMZN) 31.5x and Sea Limited’s (SE) 41x. At first glance, it seems expensive, but in reality, it reflects a quality premium: MELI combines EBITDA margins in expansion with an EPS growth rate superior to 30% annually. Its profitability over capital (40.6% ROE) is nearly double Amazon’s and four times the sector’s median.

From the EV/EBITDA FWD perspective, the market values it at 25.4x, against SE’s 22.8x and AMZN’s 14.5x. The difference lies in three factors: (1) a more diversified income mix, (2) an proprietary logistic operative leverage, and (3) fintech flow stability. Those three engines sustain high multiples, but backed.

If we take the $5.3B EBITDA expected for 2026, with a slightly higher multiple of 26x, the fair value rounds to $2,200 /share. With an expansion moderated at 27x, the upside could near 25-30%. The most important thing: even in scenarios of multiple compression at global rates, MELI maintains margins and positive flow, which limits the potential fall.

Deep down, what the market pays for is not only growth but predictability. MELI became a regional proxy of digitalization and banking, with a diversified exposition to certain types of change and consumption. That’s why I consider that its premium against global peers is sustainable, and that its re – rating towards 2026 will be more an efficiency consequence than speculation.

Risks: Macro Volatility, Competition, And Regulation

Though MELI’s story is of structural growth, there are risks that shall not be ignored.

The first continues to be Argentina, a key market where the political and FX volatility could affect the demand as much as the margins. In Q3, the local deceleration was visible, and the highest anchoring costs partially impacted the profitability.

The second risk is found in Brazil, where competition became ferocious. Shopee, Temu, and Amazon intensified promotions, forcing MELI to sustain prices and free shipments in order to conserve fees. This pressures the short-term margins, though it also reinforces long-term leadership

There is also a growing regulatory risk about the fintech business: higher capital demands, controls over rates, and new laws of digital credit could limit Mercado Pago’s expansion in some countries.

We must not forget the valuation risk: even after the multiple compression, prices remain high against peers. A change in global rates or in the risk appetite within emerging companies could generate volatility in the short term.

From a quantitative view, a strengthening of the global rates or a new risk aversion cycle could reduce the P/E FWD by 10-15%, temporally affecting the fair value price. However, MELI’s operative flow (more than an annual $2B projected towards 2026) acts as a natural buffer. In Argentina, the main risk is still FX, but its relative weight over a consolidated EBITDA is lower than 15%, which limits the direct impact.

An additional point to consider is that, in aggressive price scenarios, the real difference marks the scale. In Brazil, MELI competes not only for price but also for operational capacity, such as delivery times, logistics capillarity, and the ability to absorb peaks in demand without deteriorating the quality of service. That combination, I think more than a tactical advantage, works as a natural buffer against rivals who depend on third parties to distribute. Therefore, although competitive pressure exists and will continue, in my view the internal structure of MELI allows it to move through those cycles with less friction than the rest of the market.

Conclusion: Long-Term Value Built On Short-Term Pressure

When I review the quarter numbers, I don’t see a margin compression story. I see a company continuing to grow at an impressive pace, expanding its financial ecosystem and defending its leadership position with strategy and discipline.

As we have seen on numerous occasions, the market tends to stick with the immediate. But in companies like this, many of the decisions that today seem costly are those that ensure tomorrow’s leadership.

In summary, I do not think it was a contraction quarter but an accommodation one. MELI is moving towards a more efficient growth model, where each extra investment seeks to generate net value and strengthen its competitive advantage. That’s why I believe that despite the macro volatility it faces in the region, its fundamentals (margins, profitability, and balance sheet) remain strong.

I maintain my "Buy" stance, with a 12-24 month horizon and a target price of $2,200 per share, as its ability to expand margins, sustain an ROE above 35%, and generate increasing operating flow remains a difficult combination to find even on a global scale.

data.title: SPX Technologies: Shares Are Getting Close To A Downgrade
data.description:
One interesting company that I frankly don't look at enough is SPX Technologies (SPXC). In fact, the last article that I wrote about the business was published way back in June of 2021. And although the firm has significantly outperformed the market since I rated it a "Hold" back then, many investors think that additional upside is warranted. I say this because, as management continues to push for higher revenue, profits, and cash flows, shares are trading at rather lofty multiples. In fact, even though I am not quite at the point of downgrading it to a "Sell" rating, I believe it is getting perilously close to deserving that on valuation alone. But because of how strong financial performance has been and likely will be in the near term, I think that maintaining a "Hold" rating is appropriate.

A great business at a really hefty price

Operationally speaking, I consider SPX Technologies to be a fascinating business. Management describes the enterprise as a provider of infrastructure equipment dedicated to the HVAC and detection and measurement markets. This is a global enterprise, serving customers through operations in over 15 different countries. But to really understand the business, I think that digging a bit deeper into precisely what it does is warranted.

To me, the most interesting part of the firm is the HVAC segment, with revenue here derived from package and processing cooling equipment, engineered air movement and handling solutions, residential and commercial boilers, electrical heating, and ventilation products. The company thrives by catering to a wide array of industries, including those in the residential space, the commercial space, and the industrial space. But what I find to be particularly fascinating is that it also caters to the rapidly growing data center industry.

And then we have the Detection and Measurement side of the firm, which is a bit different. Through it, management provides customers with underground pipe and cable locators, inspection and rehabilitation equipment, robotic systems, transportation systems, communication technologies, and more. Naturally, this caters to a different type of clientele, with examples being utilities, telecommunication providers and regulators, and certain government agencies.

To many investors, these two distinct operating segments might not seem like a recipe for attractive upside. But since my last article on the firm in June 2021, shares are up 253.6% compared to the 59.6% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed. This can be attributed to the rapid growth the company has seen. Revenue from 2022 through 2024, for instance, skyrocketed from $1.46 billion to $1.98 billion. And as revenue grew, so too did profitability. Net income, as an example, soared from $19.8 million to $201.8 million. Operating cash flow went from negative $115.2 million to positive $313.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $12.6 million to $307.8 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business more than doubled from $191.5 million to $421 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Growth here for the company has continued. Revenue of $1.63 billion comfortably exceeded the $1.45 billion the company reported a year ago. And not surprisingly, we saw profitability improve as well. The one exception to this was operating cash flow. But net profits, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA all moved soundly higher.

Usually when we see this kind of strong growth, it is the result of acquisition activities. And truly, there has been some of that. In January of this year, management completed its purchase of Kranze Technology Solutions, a firm that specializes in digital interoperability and tactical networking solutions that's dedicated mostly to the defense industry. This alone cost shareholders $342 million. But there have been other purchases as well. In fact, over the last three completed fiscal years, the company has allocated $879 million to purchasing other businesses. However, this year, management really stepped things up. Total purchases amounted to $445.3 million in the first nine months of 2025. This is inclusive of its purchase of Kranze Technology Solutions, but it also included the firm's acquisition of a provider of hydronic heating and cooling equipment by the name of Sigma & Omega in a deal valued at $143.6 million.

From 2022 to 2023, acquisitions added 10.8% to the top line for the HVAC segment. There was another 12% benefit from those seen in 2024 relative to 2023. And for the first nine months of this year, acquisitions added another 4.9% to the top line. But I would be lying to you if I said that was all. Organic growth has also been incredibly strong. In fact, from 2022 to 2023, organic revenue growth added 12.2% to the top line. Management attributed this to higher volumes, most of which involved cooling products in response to a more stable labor and supply chain environment, and what management described as greater plant throughput. Price increases also seem to have played a role in this organic expansion.

The great thing about this rise in revenue is that it brought with it higher profits as well. Segment income jumped from $135.5 million to $323.9 million in the last three fiscal years. And for the first six months of this year, the $264.1 million in profits generated by the unit exceeded the $232.1 million reported for last year. And what's even better is that this was not the only area of growth for the business on either the top line or the bottom line.

This leads me to the Detection and Measurement segment. This was an area where the company did not engage as much from an acquisition standpoint. Most of its growth has been organic. Thanks to a 12.4% benefit from 2022 to 2023, with management chalking that up to higher volumes of large projects in the communication technologies, transportation, and aids to navigation businesses, revenue shot up from $547.1 million to $618.9 million. There was an increase then to $619.2 million last year. And for the first nine months of this year, driven by a 7.9% pop in organic revenue and a 10.4% contribution from acquisition activities, revenue amounted to $540.7 million compared to the $456 million reported a year earlier. As the chart above illustrates, the overall rise in sales for this unit brought with it higher profitability as well.

In all likelihood, growth for the company will continue for the foreseeable future. This is backed not only by what management says but by backlog as well. In 2022, the backlog for the business amounted to $494.1 million. By the end of last year, it had increased to $657.7 million even though sales had soared. And by the third quarter of this year, acquisition activities and strong organic demand pushed backlog up to an all-time high of $944.6 million. That's well above the $631.2 million in backlog the company had a year earlier.

Management expects this to support revenue this year of between $2.225 billion and $2.275 billion. Even at the low end, that would be significantly greater than the $1.98 billion reported for last year. Adjusted earnings per share of between $6.65 and $6.80 should mean net profits of somewhere around $329 million. And according to management, we are looking at EBITDA coming in at between $495 million and $515 million. Given what we have seen already for the year, adjusted operating cash flow, which strips out changes in working capital, should be somewhere in the neighborhood of $418.8 million.

All of this is great, and it does bring the trading multiple of the company down compared to if we were to use results from last year. But even with this growth, shares of the company are looking very pricey. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is currently valued. Normally, these are multiples where I start heavily considering a bearish assessment. But I do provide some additional flexibility for high-quality companies and firms that are growing rapidly. The fact of the matter is that SPX Technologies does check both of these boxes. However, it is important to be mindful of the fact that, in addition to being expensive on an absolute basis, shares are also expensive compared to other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such enterprises. And what I found was that it was more expensive than four of them across the board.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
SPX Technologies 32.6 25.6 21.8
Crane Company (CR) 34.0 27.9 22.8
Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) 21.1 13.3 9.7
Donaldson Company (DCI) 27.7 24.3 15.3
Mueller Industries (MLI) 16.0 16.0 11.2
Flowserve Corporation (FLS) 20.2 13.1 16.1

Takeaway

The way I see it, things are going really well for SPX Technologies. But I worry about its valuation. Coming into writing this article, I thought about possibly taking a bearish stance considering its valuation. But I'm not there just yet. The fact of the matter is that shares are very expensive, but some of that price can be justified because of the high quality of the business and management's success in growing both organically and through acquisition activities. However, I believe that a potential downgrade could happen if fundamentals falter and/or the price rises much more.

data.title: Employment Report: 119K Jobs Added In September, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 119,000 jobs were added in September, up from the loss of 4,000 in August and higher than the expected 53,000 addition. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate rose to 4.4%, its highest level since October 2021.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment edged up by 119,000 in September but has shown little change since April, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. The unemployment rate, at 4.4 percent, changed little in September. Employment continued to trend up in health care, food services and drinking places, and social assistance. Job losses occurred in transportation and warehousing and in federal government.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.4 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.6 million, changed little in September. These measures are higher than a year earlier, when the jobless rate was 4.1 percent, and the number of unemployed people was 6.9 million.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment edged up by 119,000 in September but has shown little change since April. In September, employment continued to trend up in health care, food services and drinking places, and social assistance. Job losses occurred in transportation and warehousing and in federal government.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last four years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 62,000.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 109,000, its lowest level since the initial aftermath of the pandemic.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over.

The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.440% (to three decimal places), the highest level since October 2021.

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.44%, higher than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.19%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong.

The most recent trough occurred 27 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but has since trended downward. It now stands at 1.1%, the highest level since December 2021.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 24.1 weeks, down from 24.5 weeks in August. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks.

The U6 series currently stands at 8.0%, down from 8.1% in August.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.4%, up slightly from the previous month.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 7.1%, the highest level since October 2021.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans.

In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. Since then, it has been gradually recovering toward its pre-pandemic range, with the latest reading at 59.7%, up slightly from the previous month.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM).

data.title: Ross Stores: Solid Q3 Justifies Premium Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Ross Stores, Inc. (ROST) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 15%. During a difficult environment for many consumer-facing companies, Ross has benefited from its value-orientation as consumers (particularly at lower- and middle-income levels) carefully watch discretionary spending. This momentum continued in the third quarter, sending shares up 3% in late trading on Thursday. With updated financials and shares sitting near a 52-week high, now is a good time to see if Ross has further upside or if the good news is in the price.

In the company’s third quarter, Ross Stores earned $1.58, which beat expectations by $0.16 as revenue jumped 10% to $5.6 billion. Same-store sales were up an excellent 7%, aided by a strong back-to-school shopping season. The company continues to do an excellent job of appealing to shoppers even in a difficult retail environment, thanks to its value-orientation. Additionally, it has managed through tariff costs fairly well, despite the fact that apparel faces among the most tariff pressure of any category given how much is imported from Asia.

The company operates over 2,200 stores across 44 states with California, Texas, and Florida is primary exposures. Its flagship Ross Dress of Less brand is the nation’s largest off-price apparel retailer. As you can see below, its business mix is fairly diversified across men’s, women’s, children, and home goods. 70% of shoppers are women (both shopping for themselves and their family). It has a similar “treasure hunt” experience model, typically refreshing merchandise every month or so, leading to frequent visits by its core customer. Between the success at Ross and The TJX Companies (TJX), this has proven to be a durable and attractive retail model for both apparel and home goods.

Overall, there were about $0.05 of tariff costs in the quarter, as Ross was able to pass on much, but not all of its tariff costs. Gross margins were 28% in the quarter, down about 30bps from last year. Between concessions from supplies and price increases, Ross has been able to offset over 90% of tariff costs in my estimation. Barring a change in tariff policy, I expect tariff costs to peak in Q4/Q1. A potential Supreme Court ruling to overturn tariffs could alleviate this pressure, though the Trump Administration could seek to use other legal authority to impose other tariffs. For now, I assume existing tariffs remain in effect through the remainder of the Trump Administration.

SG&A was up 10.6% to $920 million. This was consistent with revenue growth and is due to its growing store count and ongoing wage and cost inflation. Overall, Ross generated an 11.6% operating margin, down 30bps from last year, given the lower gross margin. Still, the ability to retain margins despite cost pressures and a sluggish consumer environment has been very encouraging, speaking to ongoing market share gains.

Alongside results, the company raised its full year EPS guidance to $6.38-$6.46, which includes about $0.16 of net tariff costs. This guidance implies $1.77-$1.85 in Q4 EPS and same-store sales growth of 3-4% for the year. This outlook is consistent with the current $1.81 Q4 consensus and points to a fairly healthy holiday shopping season. To the extent consumers are pulling back, it is not apparent in Ross’s results.

As we think about the consumer spending outlook beyond just Q4, the “K-shaped” economic trend bears watching, where upper-income consumption outpaces spending by lower-income cohorts. According to Bank of America’s (BAC) analysis, we are seeing this play out. As you can see below, spending has diverged with higher-income spending accelerating over the past year while lower-income spending has decelerated. Spending across cohorts though has recovered over the past few months after the initial shock of “Liberation Day,” which caused extreme market volatility.

Given the fact lower income households face more pressure from inflation and have less benefit from strong asset prices, I do expect their spending to lag. That said, within constrained budgets, we may see spending shift to more off-price retailers, providing ample opportunity for ROST to gain wallet share. It is also interesting to note where this “K-shaped” divergence is largest. It is primarily in highly discretionary sectors, like travel and leisure. There is a gap in clothing and general merchandise, but the variance is more manageable.

Given its off-price focus, results thus far, and my view a recession is unlikely, I expect ROST to continue to generate sales growth in the current environment, primarily due market share gains. Additionally, I do expect lower and middle-income households to receive some benefit in Q1 from a larger tax refund season. Changes this year to tax on tips, overtime, charitable giving, and other measures do not impact this year’s withholding and instead will be felt via larger tax refunds. This should create a $15+ billion larger tax refund season. In essence, we have not felt the economic benefits of many of this year’s tax cuts, but that should help buoy spending in 2026. This is a key reason I do not see spending rolling over further.

As it continues to open new stores, Ross has been investing meaningfully in cap-ex, spending $619 million, up from $514 million last year. Even with this level of spending, the company has generated about $1.3 billion of free cash flow year to date, despite over $100 million of working capital pressures. Depending on Q4 working capital movements, Ross is well-positioned to generate over $1.7 billion of free cash flow this year. I expect cap-ex spending to remain elevated as management sees scope to grow its store count over time by about two-thirds from current levels. Over the past year, its store count is up 3.7% to 2,273. Barring a meaningful economic downturn, I expect mid-single-digit store count growth over the medium term.

Ross ended the quarter with $3.13 billion of inventory. This is up about 9.5% from last year, which is essentially in-line with its sales growth. This is a healthy level of inventory and does not leave the company exposed to having excess product, which can force a retailer to increase promotional activity, sacrificing margins.

Capital returns have been a critical part of the Ross Stores investment thesis. Importantly, it has a strong balance sheet, enabling ongoing returns of its free cash flow to shareholders. Ross ended the quarter with $4.1 billion of cash. Against this, it has $1.5 billion of debt and $3.7 billion of operating leases. With just ~$1 billion of net debt and debt-like instruments, the balance sheet is pristine and provides ROST with significant operating and financial flexibility.

Thanks to this, in the third quarter, Ross repurchased $262 million in stock, leaving it on track to spend $1.05 billion on buybacks this year, as previously communicated. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 2% from last year. Aside from its buyback, it pays a 1% dividend yield. Given the health of its balance sheet, I see scope for higher buybacks next year after it completes its 2-year $2.1 billion buyback program.

Overall, Ross Stores continues to perform very well, and its off-price/value focus leaves it well positioned in this muted consumer spending environment. That said with shares at a 52-week high, much is in the price. Given modest sales growth and continued store openings, I am looking for the company to earn about $7 next year. That leaves shares with a ~23.5x multiple and a ~3.5% free cash flow yield. This is by no means a cheap value. However with 3-4% store growth and 2-3% same-store sales growth, we should see ~5-6% long-term growth.

This essentially paves the way for long-term returns in the 8-10% area (free cash flow yield plus growth). In other words, ROST is deserving of this premium multiple and should be able to deliver at least market-like (~8%) returns over the medium term. Plus with its pristine balance sheet and defensive macro positioning, its business model is durable, which should enable growing capital returns. As such, I view shares as a suitable Hold with Q3 results justifying the rally. I do not see enough upside to merit a buy rating (where I target a 10+% return), but on a pullback below $155, I would add Ross Stores.

data.title: Abrdn Life Sciences Investors Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The equity portion of the Fund rose (gross of fees) and outperformed its benchmark, the NASDAQ Biotechnology Index.1

The Fund outperformed its benchmark in the third quarter, driven by strong stock selection in biotechnology and therapeutics. Despite healthcare trailing broader markets due to rotation into cyclicals and AI-exposed technology, the Fund's focus on late-stage clinical catalysts and well- capitalised firms drove the outperformance. In terms of individual stocks, uniQure (QURE) advanced on renewed gene-therapy optimism, with progress in its CSL Behring partnership and programmes for Huntington's disease and epilepsy. Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) rose on strong Amvuttra uptake and cardiovascular ribonucleic acid (RNA) platform updates, with reaffirmed growth targets. Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) benefited from positive data readouts and speculative mergers and acquisitions interest in the RNA sector.

Detractors included ARS Pharmaceuticals (ASRX), which lagged due to slower product launch metrics, and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), which saw profit-taking and competitive concerns in cystic fibrosis. Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical (RARE) weakened slightly due to longer regulatory timelines in rare diseases.

The Expense Limitation Agreement may not be terminated before October 27, 2025, without the approval of the Fund's trustees who are not "interested persons" of the Fund (as defined in the 1940 Act).

Market review

US equities performed well over the quarter. The mid- August extension of the US-China tariff truce helped support sentiment, while new agreements with the European Union and Japan reduced tariffs on most of their products to 15%. However, optimism was tempered by President Trump's announcement of 100% tariffs on branded and patented pharmaceutical imports and higher duties on goods from Canada, India and Brazil, alongside expanded steel and aluminium tariffs. Monetary policy also shaped investor sentiment. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered the federal funds rate by 25 basis points to 4.00%-4.25%, describing it as a "risk management" cut. Chair Jerome Powell reaffirmed the Fed's independence and commitment to returning inflation to target while noting signs of a softer labour market. The quarter ended with a partial US government shutdown after Congress failed to agree a funding deal, weighing on short-term growth and delaying key data releases.

The performance of the US healthcare sector notably lagged that of the broader US equity market over the quarter. Cyclical sectors such as communication services, information technology and consumer discretionary outperformed as investors rotated towards growth areas given softening economic data and slowing inflation, which fuelled expectations of interest-rate cuts. Materials underperformed the broader market despite improved US-China trade sentiment after the extension of a 90-day tariff freeze, as weakness in domestic industrial demand offset any benefit, although higher gold prices reflected ongoing economic and geopolitical uncertainty. In contrast, defensive sectors including consumer staples, utilities and real estate underperformed as risk appetite increased. Financials and industrials likewise underperformed, held back by softer economic data and waning business confidence. Energy broadly matched the performance of US equities, with oil prices ending flat as signs of softening demand, reflected in rising crude inventories, were offset by geopolitical tensions that raised supply concerns.

Activity

The Fund added positions in Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ), Arcus Biosciences (RCUS), Aquestive Therapeutics (AQST), Equillium (EQ), Shattuck Labs (STTK) and Apogee Therapeutics (APGE), targeting firms with strong balance sheets and near-term catalysts. Trims in Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals locked in gains. Smaller biotechnology positions with matured theses or liquidity concerns were sold, increasing exposure to late- stage pipelines and diversifying the therapeutic mix.

Outlook & strategy

Uncertainty continues to cloud the macroeconomic, geopolitical and market outlook. A combination of shifting fiscal and monetary policies along with structural changes in global trade dynamics has created an increasingly volatile market environment. Much of the focus remains on the Trump administration's tariff strategy and its potential implications for economic growth and inflation. However, the broader macroeconomic backdrop is further complicated by labour-market data revisions, proposed tax cuts, widening budget deficits, evolving interest-rate policy, healthcare reform and a range of other policy initiatives that may have both intended and unintended consequences. Given the rapidly evolving landscape, market participants are struggling to build meaningful conviction around the trajectory of both domestic and international economies.

In general, the near- and long-term outlooks for healthcare companies remain favourable. Long-term demographic trends of an aging population should continue to support the growing demand for new healthcare products and therapies. Due to the healthcare sector's generally defensive characteristics, its relative underperformance in the short term should position it favourably, especially if the US and global economies slow down.
